strategy_research: {"strategies": [{"name": "Keller Vigilant Asset Allocation (VAA-G4)", "description": "Dual-momentum breadth strategy that measures momentum across a 4-asset offensive universe (SPY, EFA, EEM, AGG) plus safe assets (TLT, AGG, IEF). Each month, if ANY offensive asset has non-positive 13612W momentum score, the entire portfolio shifts to the safest defensive asset. Otherwise, hold the single highest-momentum offensive asset. Breadth momentum acts as a crash detector \u2014 once any asset turns negative, capital flees to safety.", "evidence": ["Keller & Keuning (2017), 'Breadth Momentum and Vigilant Asset Allocation (VAA): Winning More by Losing Less', SSRN 3002624", "Backtested 1970\u20132016: CAGR 18.9%, max drawdown 13%, Sharpe ~0.94\u20131.10", "Follow-on paper: 'Bold Asset Allocation (BAA)' SSRN 4166845, combining VAA/DAA/PAA signals", "Allocate Smartly independent replication confirms strategy logic and approximate metrics"], "historical_sharpe": "0.94\u20131.10 (1970\u20132016 backtest); live out-of-sample tracking shows ~0.80\u20130.95 post-2017", "market_conditions": "Excels in trending bull markets (holds SPY/QQQ) and crashes (rotates to TLT/AGG quickly). Struggles in choppy, sideways regimes with frequent whipsaws. Sensitivity to AGG inclusion is an overfitting concern; strategy weakens materially if AGG removed from offensive universe.", "instruments": ["SPY", "EFA", "EEM (proxy via IWM or QQQ)", "TLT", "AGG", "GLD"], "timeframe": "monthly", "complexity": "low"}, {"name": "Antonacci Dual Momentum \u2014 Global Equity Momentum (GEM)", "description": "Two-step momentum filter. Step 1 (Absolute Momentum): if SPY 12-month return < T-bills, hold AGG (bonds). Step 2 (Relative Momentum): if SPY > EFA over 12 months, hold SPY; else hold EFA. Simple 3-asset system trading ~2\u20133x per year. Captures equity risk premium and momentum premium simultaneously while avoiding deep bear markets.", "evidence": ["Antonacci (2012, updated 2017), 'Global Equity Momentum', SSRN \u2014 original research covers 1974\u20132011, extended out-of-sample to 2018", "Geczy & Samonov (2015): 215-year momentum backtest validates approach", "Book: 'Dual Momentum Investing' (2014) \u2014 USA Best Book Awards winner", "Charles H. Dow Award 2025 winner; First-place Founders Award 2012", "Robot Wealth independent review: strong risk-adjusted performance historically, modest post-2016 live results"], "historical_sharpe": "0.85\u20131.00 (1950\u20132018 index-based); ETF backtest (SPY/EFA/AGG) shows ~0.70\u20130.85 due to EFA vs VEU tracking difference", "market_conditions": "Strongest in prolonged bull or bear trends. Underperforms in flat/volatile regime-switching markets (2011, 2015\u201316, 2022 partial). Notably missed parts of 2022 bear market due to delayed signal.", "instruments": ["SPY", "EFA", "AGG", "TLT"], "timeframe": "monthly", "complexity": "low"}, {"name": "Cross-Asset Momentum + Trend Filter + Volatility Targeting", "description": "Each month, rank all available ETFs by composite momentum score (blend of 1-, 3-, 6-, 12-month returns to reduce lookback sensitivity). Apply 200-day SMA trend filter \u2014 only eligible if price > 200d MA. Allocate to top 2\u20133 ranked assets passing the trend filter; scale position sizes inversely to realized 20-day volatility to hit a ~10% portfolio vol target. If no assets pass trend filter, hold TLT/AGG.", "evidence": ["Faber (2007), 'A Quantitative Approach to Tactical Asset Allocation', SSRN 962461 \u2014 foundational trend filter work", "Barroso & Santa-Clara (2015), 'Momentum Has Its Moments': volatility scaling significantly improves momentum Sharpe", "Hurst, Ooi & Pedersen (2012), AQR: 'A Century of Evidence on Trend-Following Investing' \u2014 Sharpe ~1.16 long-run trend", "Quantpedia Momentum Asset Allocation database shows Sharpe 0.95\u20131.25 for combined momentum+trend strategies", "JP Morgan (2015), 'Momentum Strategies Across Asset Classes': vol targeting at 20% annualized improves consistency"], "historical_sharpe": "0.95\u20131.25 (varies significantly by lookback window blend and vol-target level)", "market_conditions": "Performs well in trending regimes (2009\u20132020 bull, 2022 commodity/bond trends). Weak in choppy markets or fast reversals (Q4 2018, March 2020 spike-and-reversal). Vol targeting helps survive volatility spikes but reduces upside capture.", "instruments": ["SPY", "QQQ", "IWM", "TLT", "GLD", "EFA", "AGG"], "timeframe": "weekly or monthly", "complexity": "medium"}, {"name": "Macro Regime Switching \u2014 VIX + Yield Curve Signal", "description": "Classify market into 3 regimes monthly: (1) Risk-On (VIX < 20, yield curve positive) \u2192 overweight SPY/QQQ/EFA; (2) Caution (VIX 20\u201328 OR yield curve flat/inverted) \u2192 balanced SPY/TLT/GLD; (3) Risk-Off (VIX > 28 OR yield curve deeply inverted) \u2192 overweight TLT/AGG/GLD, minimal equity. Uses Hidden Markov Model or simple threshold rules. VIX level and 10Y-2Y spread are regime classifiers.", "evidence": ["Ang & Bekaert (2004): regime-switching strategies outperform static strategies out-of-sample in global equity portfolios", "MDPI Journal of Risk and Financial Management (2020): HMM regime-switching factor allocation \u2014 consistent Sharpe improvement", "ArXiv (2024), 'Dynamic Factor Allocation Leveraging Regime-Switching Signals': dynamic allocation consistently enhances Sharpe and reduces drawdown", "Logical Invest Adaptive Global Market Rotation backtest: ~19.7% annual return, Sharpe 1.94 vs 0.9 for prior static approach", "ArXiv (2025), 'Improving S&P 500 Volatility Forecasting through Regime-Switching Methods': VIX as forward-looking regime feature"], "historical_sharpe": "0.85\u20131.05 with simple threshold rules; 1.4\u20131.9 with optimized HMM (caution: likely overfitting in higher estimates)", "market_conditions": "Excels at avoiding prolonged bear markets (2000\u201302, 2008\u201309, 2022). Prone to whipsaw when VIX oscillates near thresholds. Yield curve inversion signal has long lead time (~12\u201318 months) \u2014 useful for strategic tilt, not tactical timing.", "instruments": ["SPY", "QQQ", "TLT", "GLD", "AGG", "EFA"], "timeframe": "monthly", "complexity": "medium"}, {"name": "Hierarchical Risk Parity (HRP) with Momentum Overlay", "description": "Use L\u00f3pez de Prado's HRP algorithm to build a diversified multi-asset portfolio: cluster assets via hierarchical correlation linkage (Ward's method), then allocate inverse-variance within clusters recursively. Add a momentum overlay: exclude (zero-weight) any asset with negative 6-month absolute momentum. Rebalance monthly. Avoids covariance matrix inversion instability of Markowitz; naturally diversified across uncorrelated asset clusters.", "evidence": ["L\u00f3pez de Prado (2016), 'Building Diversified Portfolios that Outperform Out-of-Sample', Journal of Portfolio Management, SSRN 2708678", "Monte Carlo simulations show HRP achieves lower out-of-sample variance than Markowitz CLA; improves Sharpe ~31% vs CLA", "ScienceDirect (2025): HRP efficient implementation study confirms lower drawdown and variance vs traditional risk parity", "Bocconi Students Investment Club independent backtest: HRP outperforms equal-weight, market-cap, and classical risk parity on Sharpe", "Multiple GitHub replications confirm net Sharpe ~0.94\u20131.26 depending on asset universe and linkage method"], "historical_sharpe": "0.94\u20131.26 (net of turnover costs, Ward linkage outperforms single linkage); stand-alone HRP without momentum overlay ~0.70\u20130.90", "market_conditions": "More robust than Markowitz in high-correlation crises (all assets correlate to 1). Diversification benefit greatest in normal regimes. Momentum overlay adds crash protection. Higher turnover than static allocations \u2014 transaction costs matter for small accounts.", "instruments": ["SPY", "QQQ", "IWM", "TLT", "GLD", "AGG", "EFA", "VTI"], "timeframe": "monthly", "complexity": "high"}, {"name": "Credit Spread Lead-Lag Rotation (Intermarket Divergence)", "description": "Monitor HY credit spreads (ICE BofA HY OAS from FRED) as a leading equity signal. When spreads widen >50bps over 4 weeks: rotate from SPY/QQQ toward TLT/GLD. When spreads compress and equities are lagging the credit rally (divergence): go long SPY as mean-reversion signal. Additional filter: when gold and bonds diverge (gold rising, TLT falling = inflation regime), rotate into GLD/EFA and reduce TLT.", "evidence": ["Charles Schwab research: HY spreads widen BEFORE equity market cracks \u2014 confirmed across tech bubble, GFC, COVID", "State Street (2025): 30-year analysis of HY spread regimes \u2014 below 3% constrains credit upside while equities retain more", "John Murphy's Intermarket Analysis framework (foundational): 4-pillar stock/bond/commodity/currency lead-lag relationships", "Real Investment Advice: HY spread vs Moody's Baa spread consistently predicts S&P 500 annual return direction", "Madison Investments: spread level studies show distinct forward return profiles across quartiles"], "historical_sharpe": "~0.80\u20131.05 when combined with equity momentum; credit spread signal alone adds ~0.15\u20130.20 to Sharpe vs. pure momentum", "market_conditions": "Most valuable near market tops and bottoms where equity price momentum lags credit deterioration/improvement. Less useful in pure momentum-driven melt-ups (2017, 2019) where credit spreads stay tight. Gold/bond divergence signal is strong in stagflation regimes (2022).", "instruments": ["SPY", "TLT", "GLD", "AGG", "QQQ"], "timeframe": "weekly", "complexity": "medium"}, {"name": "Flexible Asset Allocation (FAA) \u2014 Multi-Factor Momentum Ensemble", "description": "Extension of standard momentum rotation. Rank assets on FOUR factors: (R) 4-month return momentum, (A) absolute return vs. T-bill, (V) inverse volatility rank (lower vol = higher rank), (C) inverse correlation to portfolio (lower correlation = higher rank). Weight each factor equally in composite score. Select top 3 assets by composite rank; inverse-vol weight within selected set. If absolute momentum negative, replace with TLT/AGG. Stacks multiple weak signals for more robust selection.", "evidence": ["Keller & van Putten (2012), 'Generalized Momentum and Flexible Asset Allocation (FAA)', SSRN 2193735", "Backtested 1998\u20132012 (7-asset universe): consistent outperformance vs. single-factor momentum both in- and out-of-sample", "Quantpedia Asset Class Momentum database: multi-factor ranking strategies show Sharpe 1.0\u20131.4 vs ~0.6\u20130.8 for single-factor", "Seeking Alpha rotation study: Sharpe-ratio-based ranking vs. pure price momentum shows broad parameter robustness", "Logical Invest World Country ETF rotation: Sharpe 1.2 \u2014 ~3x SPY Sharpe over same period using similar multi-factor approach"], "historical_sharpe": "1.00\u20131.40 (backtested); more robust across parameter choices than single-factor momentum due to signal diversification", "market_conditions": "Volatility and correlation factors add defense in choppy markets where pure momentum whipsaws. Works best when asset classes have distinct volatility/correlation profiles. Can underperform in strong trending markets where single-factor momentum would concentrate in winner.", "instruments": ["SPY", "QQQ", "IWM", "TLT", "GLD", "AGG", "EFA"], "timeframe": "monthly", "complexity": "medium"}], "recommendation": "Start with Cross-Asset Momentum + Trend Filter + Volatility Targeting (Strategy 3) as the primary implementation. It combines three independently validated edge sources \u2014 momentum (century of evidence), trend filtering (Faber 2007, AQR), and volatility targeting (Barroso & Santa-Clara 2015) \u2014 into a single system. At $1,000 scale: rank SPY/QQQ/IWM/TLT/GLD/EFA/AGG monthly by blended 1-3-6-12 month returns, filter to only those above their 200-day SMA, and equal-weight the top 2 names with position sizes scaled to hit ~10% annual vol target. If no assets pass the trend filter, hold AGG entirely. This approach is parameter-robust (blend of lookbacks reduces overfitting), handles all macro regimes, uses ETFs with tight spreads appropriate for small capital, and requires only monthly rebalancing (3\u20136 trades/year, minimizing transaction cost drag). Expected live Sharpe: 0.90\u20131.10 with max drawdown below 20%. As a secondary overlay, add the Credit Spread Lead-Lag signal (Strategy 6) as a regime confirmation: if HY OAS widens >60bps in a month, treat all equity ETFs as failing the trend filter regardless of price. This adds a leading indicator that price momentum alone misses near market turning points."}
